topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy. Mrs. Henderson, next door, had asked if I could let her use a section of my overgrown patch of lawn for her vegetable plot. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead feeling like a physical pressure on my temples. Every time I thought about it, a familiar weight settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and started typing a reply, but the words felt sluggish, like trying to move through thick mud. I just wanted the whole thing to disappear.

[story 2]

When Mr. Peterson knocked on my door, his smile was wide and entirely too bright for the grey afternoon. He spoke about his desire for a little green space, a patch of earth for tomatoes and herbs, right near the fence line. I managed a tight nod, my gaze fixed on a crack in the driveway. Internally, a cold numbness spread through my limbs. I stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling utterly drained, as if I had already run a marathon I couldn't finish. "Well," I mumbled, the sound barely audible, "I suppose I could look at it." The effort of simply agreeing felt monumental.

[story 3]

The request arrived tucked into my mailbox, a brightly colored flyer detailing Mrs. Gable’s ambitious plans for a new flower bed. Reading the neat handwriting, I felt a sudden, sharp tightening in my jaw. I traced the outline of the request with my finger, the paper crinkling under the pressure. Outside, the sun beat down, and I found myself staring blankly at the weeds pushing through the cracked paving stones. A profound sense of inertia held me captive; the energy required to even consider digging up the soil seemed impossibly vast. I sighed, a long, slow release of air that didn't seem to carry any actual relief.
